title: 🎙️ Working Lands & Industry
description: Today's focus is the resource economy that defines the Cariboo — forestry, ranching, mining, and the agricultural technology helping these industries adapt and modernize. We're looking for stories about the people and innovations keeping rural BC's working landscape viable, including tensions between resource extraction and long-term stewardship, and the technology bridging both. - Daily themed article selection for podcast generation

_podcast.theme_description: Today's focus is the resource economy that defines the Cariboo — forestry, ranching, mining, and the agricultural technology helping these industries adapt and modernize. We're looking for stories about the people and innovations keeping rural BC's working landscape viable, including tensions between resource extraction and long-term stewardship, and the technology bridging both.
_podcast.theme_scoring_prompt:
Rate how well this article fits the theme 'Working Lands & Industry' — the resource economy that defines the Cariboo and rural BC.

CALIBRATION — use this ladder. Trade-press journalism about forestry, ranching, mining, or agriculture should score well even when it's equipment-, market-, or operations-focused rather than policy-focused — a story about a piece of farm machinery or a mining operation update is a real fit, not a 0:
- 80-100: BC/Cariboo-specific forestry, ranching, mining, or agriculture stories (mill closures, beetle kill, BCTS, Cariboo ranchers and water rights, local mine operations, Cariboo Cattlemen events).
- 55-79: Canadian resource-economy trade journalism without a BC tie — Western Producer or Northern Miner-style coverage of farm equipment and machinery, harvest technology, livestock markets, mining operations and exploration, AgTech, forestry practices, sawmills, or labour/commodity stories in the resource sector.
- 30-54: Broader agriculture, forestry, ranching, or mining news (e.g. international resource-industry stories, general farm economics, climate impacts on working lands) with clear working-lands relevance but no Canadian/BC tie.
- 15-29: Tangential resource-sector mentions (e.g. a general business, policy, or science story that touches briefly on forestry/agriculture/mining) or local Cariboo civic news unrelated to working lands.
- 0-14: No connection to forestry, ranching, mining, or agriculture.

Strong fits include: BC and Canadian forestry journalism (mill closures, lumber markets, beetle kill, harvest operations, silviculture, BCTS, old-growth policy), ranching and livestock stories (water rights, grazing management, cattle markets, drought impact on ranches), mining and resource extraction (safety, new operations, Indigenous consultation, supply chains, exploration updates), agriculture in rural Canada (crop insurance, grain prices, farm economics, food sovereignty, farm equipment and machinery), and the technology helping these industries adapt — precision forestry, AgTech, drone surveys, IoT in agriculture, mine automation, sawmill modernization, climate adaptation on working lands. Also strong fits: labor and workforce stories in resource industries, environmental stewardship within working landscapes (not pure conservation, but how ranchers/loggers manage for sustainability), and science with direct implications for forestry, agriculture, or extractive industries. Penalise heavily (score 0–14): international geopolitical and war news with no resource-economy connection; consumer tech and gadget reviews; pure wildlife entertainment without agricultural or working-lands relevance; crime and judicial news unrelated to resource industries; financial market and stock ticker stories. Score 0-100 where 100 is a perfect thematic match and 0 is completely unrelated.

items.title: [Boing Boing] Germany told parents to destroy this talking doll
items.content_html:
🌾 ✏️

In 2014, a talking doll named My Friend Cayla was voted Innovative Toy of the Year. Three years later the German government was telling parents to destroy it. Cayla listened to a child through a hidden microphone, beamed the audio over Bluetooth to a phone app, searched the internet for an answer, and spoke back after about a one-second delay. — Read the rest

The post Germany told parents to destroy this talking doll appeared first on Boing Boing.

items.title: [Atlas Obscura] Ike Towell’s Grave in Columbus, Texas
items.content_html:
🌾 ✏️

Isaac "Ike" Towell was born on November 25, 1849 and died, by suicide, on February 22, 1934 at the age of 83 in Columbus, Texas.  

Ike was the city marshal of Columbus in 1885 and a lifelong "infidel".  He was known as a man of unwavering principals and had many friends near and far.  An outspoken atheist, he was recognized for his uncompromising honesty, his opposition to alcohol and tobacco, and his fearless, courageous nature. 

About a year before his death, he arranged for a headstone to be placed at his future gravesite.  A few days before he passed away, he purchased his own coffin and left detailed instructions regarding his funeral arrangements.  The service consisted of a final message written by Mr. Towell and was directed to be read by someone "not afraid that lightening would strike during the service".

Over 100 people were present at his service to hear the following message:

"Especially do I want to impress the fact upon the people that one child was born, passed through the years of youth, grew to manhood, lived a tolerably respectable life, reached old age, died and could be buried without the assistance of the clergy."

"It is my will and I so direct that my funeral be the cheapest possible. I do not hold with carrying a person from county to county for the sole purpose of burial.

"My religion consists of doing right and loving justice. I affirm that all men should tell the truth and pay their debts. I do not believe in any God, devil, ghost or savior and I have opposed tobacco, whiskey, gambling, lying and stealing practically all my life. If any one of the clergy ever calls my name after death I insist that he speak the truth about me, a thing I have never known a preacher to do about a dead disbeliever. The clergy have never been able to bribe me with the promise of a beautiful home in a fictitious heaven nor bluff my by their everlasting punishment in a hell of fire and brimstone."

Headstone Inscription:

"HERE RESTS
IKE TOWELL
AN INFIDEL WHO HAD
NO HOPE OF HEAVEN
NOR FEAR OF HELL
WAS FREE OF SUPERSTITION
TO DO RIGHT AND LOVE
JUSTICE WAS HIS RELIGION"
items.summary: Isaac "Ike" Towell was born on November 25, 1849 and died, by suicide, on February 22, 1934 at the age of 83 in Columbus, Texas. Ike was the city marshal of Columbus in 1885 and a lifelong "infidel". He was known as a man of unwavering principals and had many friends near and far. An outspoken

items.title: [Atlas Obscura] La Casa del Serpentello in Vignale Monferrato, Italy
items.content_html:
🌾 ✏️

What makes the Serpentello House truly special is its unique artistic character. The façade and many of its decorations are entirely covered with handcrafted ceramic tiles, each one unique and personally designed and created by the owner, Luciano Serpentello, in 1925.

This remarkable attention to detail transforms the house into a true work of art, giving it a distinctive and unforgettable atmosphere.

Non v'è arte né simmetria,
Unico pregio la fantasia.
In pochi anni di mia vita
Da me quest'opera venne compita.
Se non bella è originale
Ed è difficile trovar l'eguale.
Un contadino vi ha posto mano,
Tal Serpentello signor Luciano.

Translated by Google:

No art or symmetry is found here,
Its only merit is imagination.
In but a few years of my life,
I brought this work to completion.
If not beautiful, it is original,
And its equal is hard to find.
A farmer set his hand to it—
Signor Luciano, known as "Serpentello."

As the inscription above suggests, the Serpentello House is often regarded as a "visual encyclopedia" of rural life and the boundless imagination of its creator.

items.title: [Atlas Obscura] Fuel Tank Orca Mural in Palmer Station, Antarctica
items.content_html:
🌾 ✏️

The fuel tank mural at Palmer Station is one of the most recognizable landmarks at this remote Antarctic research base. Painted on the station’s large fuel tank, the mural features a leaping orca and adds a unique piece of public art to the otherwise industrial scenery. Visible from the water as ships approach the station, it has become a familiar symbol of Palmer Station for residents and visitors alike.

The mural stands out against the surrounding Antarctic landscape and serves as a reminder that even in one of the world’s most isolated workplaces, people bring creativity and personality to their surroundings.

items.title: [Boing Boing] Enter Rachel Lichtman's groovy, shag-carpeted world of Golden Sands
items.content_html:
🌾 ✏️

I just discovered, through Frank Santopadre's podcast Fun for All Ages, the radio and television universe of artist Rachel Lichtman's imagined 1960s/1970s town of Golden Sands.

It's a surreal town stuck in a mid-century far out brown-and-orange aesthetic. Lichtman creates parody television commercials, and print and radio ads from the town of Golden Sands, as well as opening credit theme song videos from the town's television station, Programme 4. — Read the rest

The post Enter Rachel Lichtman's groovy, shag-carpeted world of Golden Sands appeared first on Boing Boing.

items.title: [ScienceDaily] Scientists revive a powerful antibiotic that superbugs had defeated
items.content_html:
🌾 ✏️

Scientists have found a way to revive vancomycin, a powerful antibiotic that some dangerous bacteria have learned to resist. Instead of creating an entirely new drug, researchers paired vancomycin with a small molecule called pghi-4, which blocks a bacterial enzyme linked to resistance. The combination restored the antibiotic’s ability to kill drug-resistant E. faecium, raising hopes that similar chemical helpers could rescue other failing medicines.
items.summary: Scientists have found a way to revive vancomycin, a powerful antibiotic that some dangerous bacteria have learned to resist. Instead of creating an entirely new drug, researchers paired vancomycin with a small molecule called pghi-4, which blocks a bacterial enzyme linked to resistance. The
items._excerpt: Scientists have found a way to revive vancomycin, a powerful antibiotic that some dangerous bacteria have learned to resist. The combination restored the antibiotic’s ability to kill drug-resistant E. faecium, raising hopes that similar chemical helpers could rescue other failing medicines.

items.title: [The Marginalian] How Not to Waste Your Life
items.content_html:
🌾 ✏️

“Let me not seem to have lived in vain,” the Danish astronomer Tycho Brahe whispered on his deathbed, not realizing that the astronomical tables he was leaving behind would become the portal through which Kepler arrives at the laws of planetary motion; not realizing that the measure of an unwasted life is not what outlives it but how it was lived — how much integrity and authenticity and creative vitality filled these numbered days, these unrepeatable hours. Most of us will not leave behind a revolutionary insight into the nature of the universe, but we too forget that no matter… read article

items.title: [New Atlas] X camper van steers space-shifting wizardry deep off-road
items.content_html:
🌾 ✏️

If you ask us, off-road camping is the best kind of camping. But hours after hours of brutal, hard-hammering, slow-crawling trail driving can also leave the body aching before you even step out of the vehicle. Unfortunately, many of the best 4x4 camper vans will require you to squeeze those aching limbs into an undersized bed at night. But the new Bürstner Habiton X gives you an agile Sprinter 144 on the trail and your own personal longitudinal bed at camp. That's all thanks to its space-optimizing transformer capabilities.

Continue Reading

Category: Campervans, Adventure Vehicles, Outdoors

Tags: burstner, Campervan, Off-road, Off-grid, Hymer, Transformable, RV, Outdoors and Camping, Camping

Crop Production Show · Free Newsletter · Digital Editions · Subscribe · Proudly Canadian · Markets · Advertissement · By Adam Peleshaty · Reporter · 2 days ago · 1 min read · Farm Credit Canada gave its latest Canadian economic forecast Sept. 18. Photo: file · The Canadian dollar was softer on Monday morning amid narrowly mixed crude oil prices. The loonie was at US$0.7116 or US$1=C$1.4053 as of 8:30 a.m. CDT, compared to Friday’s close of US$0.7136 or US$1=C$1.4014.

items.title: [The Marginalian] Borges on How to Conquer Time
items.content_html:
🌾 ✏️

"Time is the substance I am made of. Time is a river which sweeps me along, but I am the river; it is a tiger which destroys me, but I am the tiger; it is a fire which consumes me, but I am the fire."
items.summary: "Time is the substance I am made of. Time is a river which sweeps me along, but I am the river; it is a tiger which destroys me, but I am the tiger; it is a fire which consumes me, but I am the fire."
